name: Applied Chemistry
description: Basic chemistry concepts relevant to engineering applications.

topics.title: Fuels: Classification and Characteristics of Fuels

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: Fuel is a combustible substance that releases useful heat on burning in oxygen.

definitions: Fuel: Substance that produces heat energy on combustion.
Primary fuel: Fuel available directly in nature (coal, petroleum, natural gas).
Secondary fuel: Fuel produced from primary fuel (coke, LPG, producer gas).
theory: Fuels are classified as solid, liquid, and gaseous. This classification is based on physical state and influences storage, handling, and combustion control.
Important characteristics include calorific value, ignition temperature, moisture content, ash content, sulfur content, volatility, and combustion efficiency.
A good fuel should have high calorific value, moderate ignition temperature, low moisture and ash, low sulfur, easy availability, and safe handling properties.

| Fuel Type | Example | Main Advantage | Main Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Solid | Coal | Cheap and widely available | Ash handling and smoke |
| Liquid | Diesel | High energy density, easy pumping | Storage/fire risk |
| Gas | NG/LPG | Clean and controllable combustion | Pressurized storage needed |
keyPoints: Exam focus: classify fuels with examples and properties.
Always mention one practical use for each class.
State ideal characteristics of a good fuel in bullet form.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: Calorific value is the amount of heat released by complete combustion of unit mass (or volume) of fuel.

definitions: HCV/GCV: Includes latent heat of condensed water vapor.
LCV/NCV: Excludes latent heat of water vapor.
theory: Calorific value indicates fuel quality and is used in boiler design, engine performance analysis, and fuel-cost comparison.
HCV is always greater than LCV because HCV assumes water formed during combustion is condensed and latent heat is recovered.
For engineering applications involving exhaust gases at high temperature, LCV is commonly used because water stays in vapor state.
keyPoints: Concept clarity + diagram/process understanding are both necessary.
Unit consistency and stepwise calculations are critical in numericals.
Examiners reward concise and structured presentation.
formulas: HCV = (W × ΔT) / m  (after corrections where applicable)
LCV = HCV − (0.09 × H × 587) kcal/kg  (H in wt% for conventional form)

examples.label: Solved Example 1
examples.problem: If HCV = 45 MJ/kg and latent heat correction = 2.2 MJ/kg, find LCV.
examples.answer: LCV = 42.8 MJ/kg

examples.label: Solved Example 2
examples.problem: Why is LCV used in gas turbine calculations?
solution: Exhaust gases leave at high temperature.
Water does not condense in practical operation.
examples.answer: Because latent heat of water vapor is practically unavailable in turbine exhaust.

topics.title: Comparison Between Solid, Liquid and Gaseous Fuels

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: This topic compares fuel classes by handling, combustion, cost, and pollution behavior.

definitions: Comparison Between Solid, Liquid and Gaseous Fuels: Core Unit-1 concept in Applied Chemistry.
theory: Solid fuels are easy to store and cheap but produce ash and particulate emissions.
Liquid fuels have better energy density and can be pumped/atomized; combustion control is easier than solids.
Gaseous fuels provide clean and almost complete combustion with excellent control, but storage/transport infrastructure is expensive.

| Parameter | Solid | Liquid | Gaseous |
|---|---|---|---|
| Combustion control | Poor | Better | Best |
| Cleanliness | Low | Medium | High |
| Handling | Bulk/Manual | Pumpable | Piping/compressed storage |
keyPoints: Write comparison in tabular form for high-scoring answers.
Mention at least 5 parameters in exams: cost, handling, storage, efficiency, pollution.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: Bomb calorimeter is a constant-volume calorimeter used for accurate determination of HCV of solid/liquid fuels.

definitions: Bomb Calorimeter: Core Unit-1 concept in Applied Chemistry.
theory: A weighed fuel sample is burned in a sealed steel bomb filled with oxygen. Heat released raises water temperature around the bomb.
From temperature rise and calorimeter constant, heat of combustion is obtained. Fuse wire and acid corrections are applied for accuracy.
Bomb calorimeter gives highly reliable HCV values and is a standard laboratory method.
keyPoints: Concept clarity + diagram/process understanding are both necessary.
Unit consistency and stepwise calculations are critical in numericals.
Examiners reward concise and structured presentation.
formulas: HCV = [(W + w) × ΔT − corrections] / m
where W = water mass, w = water equivalent, m = fuel mass

examples.label: Solved Example 1
examples.problem: Given (W+w)=2500 g, ΔT=2.5°C, correction=100 cal, m=1 g. Calculate HCV in cal/g.
examples.answer: HCV = 6150 cal/g

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: Boy's calorimeter is a flow calorimeter used primarily for gaseous fuel calorific value estimation.

definitions: Boy’s Calorimeter: Core Unit-1 concept in Applied Chemistry.
theory: Fuel burns at a burner and transfers heat to a known water flow. Temperature rise of water is measured.
It is comparatively simpler than bomb calorimeter but less accurate due to heat losses.
Used for gaseous fuels and demonstration-level practical understanding of calorimetry.
keyPoints: Concept clarity + diagram/process understanding are both necessary.
Unit consistency and stepwise calculations are critical in numericals.
Examiners reward concise and structured presentation.
formulas: Heat gained by water = mw × cw × ΔT
Calorific value = Heat gained / mass (or volume) of fuel consumed

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: Dulong formula estimates HCV from ultimate analysis data (C, H, O, S percentages).

definitions: Dulong Formula: Core Unit-1 concept in Applied Chemistry.
theory: This empirical formula provides quick theoretical calorific value without direct calorimeter experiment.
The term (H − O/8) accounts for hydrogen unavailable for combustion because some hydrogen is already bound with oxygen in fuel.
keyPoints: Concept clarity + diagram/process understanding are both necessary.
Unit consistency and stepwise calculations are critical in numericals.
Examiners reward concise and structured presentation.
formulas: C, H, O, S are weight percentages of fuel elements

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: Coal is a carbon-rich solid fossil fuel formed by geological transformation of plant matter.

definitions: Peat, lignite, bituminous coal, anthracite (in increasing rank order).
theory: Coal rank determines moisture, volatile matter, fixed carbon, and calorific value.
High-rank coals have more carbon and higher CV; low-rank coals have more moisture and lower CV.

topics.title: Phase rule: Terms used in Gibb’s Phase rule, phase diagram

theory: Gibbs' Phase Rule provides the relationship between the number of degrees of freedom (F), number of components (C) and the number of phases (P) present in a system at equilibrium. The general form is F = C - P + 2 for systems where pressure and temperature may vary; for condensed systems (fixed pressure) the formula is often written as F = C - P + 1.

Key terms: component (independent chemical species required to define the system), phase (physically distinct and mechanically separable part of the system), degree of freedom (number of independent intensive variables like T, P, composition that can be changed without changing the number of phases).

Phase diagrams graphically represent regions of stability for different phases as functions of variables such as temperature, pressure or composition. Lines represent equilibria between two phases and points where three phases coexist are called triple points.
keyPoints: Memorise Gibbs' Phase Rule and when to use the condensed form.
Understand component vs. phase with simple examples (pure substance, binary alloy).
Interpret P-T and T-composition diagrams; identify invariant and univariant regions.
examples: Apply F = C - P + 2 to a one-component system at the triple point (C=1, P=3 => F=0, invariant).
Explain why a binary eutectic is univariant along the liquidus line (F=1).
examQuestions: State Gibbs' Phase Rule and explain each term with an example.
Interpret a simple P-T diagram and identify the triple point.

theory: One-component phase behaviour shows how a pure substance occupies different phases depending on temperature and pressure. Water and sulphur are typical examples used in engineering chemistry.

Water: discuss solid–liquid–vapour equilibria, triple point (0.01°C, 0.00611 atm), and critical point (374°C, 22.06 MPa). Note anomalous density behaviour of water near 4°C which affects the slope of the melting curve.
Sulphur: describe allotropy (S8 rings, polymeric chains on heating), melting and sublimation behaviour, and the relevance of allotropes to physical properties and processing.
keyPoints: Triple point and critical point definitions (water).
Allotropic forms and transitions (sulphur).
Engineering implications: steam cycles, corrosion, storage and handling considerations.
examples: Explain why water's melting curve has a negative slope and why this is important for ice formation in natural environments.
Describe how sulphur's ring–chain transformation affects its viscosity when molten.
examQuestions: Define triple point and critical point with reference to water.
Describe the allotropes of sulphur and one engineering consequence of sulphur allotropy.

theory: Binary (two-component) phase diagrams show how composition and temperature determine phase stability. Important features include liquidus, solidus, solvus lines, eutectic and peritectic points, and regions of solid solution or two-phase mixtures.
Lead–Silver (Pb–Ag): illustrate limited solid solubility at room temperature and a simple eutectic behaviour that influences solder and casting microstructures.
Zinc–Magnesium (Zn–Mg): discuss phase separation, intermetallic formation and how small additions of Mg influence the melting and mechanical behaviour of Zn alloys.
keyPoints: Read liquidus and solidus to determine first solid formed on cooling.
Use the lever rule to compute phase fractions at a given temperature and composition.
Relate diagram features to practical metallurgy (casting, soldering, alloy selection).
examples: Apply the lever rule to a Pb–Ag alloy at a temperature between liquidus and solidus to estimate phase proportions.
Explain why eutectic alloys have a characteristic fine microstructure upon solidification.
examQuestions: Use the lever rule on a binary phase diagram to find phase percentages.
Describe eutectic solidification and its metallurgical consequences.

topics.title: Polymers: Classification, functionality and their types

theory: Polymers are large molecules made up of repeating monomer units. Classify by source (natural vs synthetic), polymerisation mechanism (addition vs condensation), and structure (linear, branched, cross-linked).

Functionality refers to the number of reactive sites on a monomer; it controls network formation (e.g., difunctional monomers give linear chains; tri- or higher-functional monomers lead to branching and cross-linking).
Introduce thermoplastics (reversible chain entanglement) vs thermosets (irreversible cross-linked networks).
keyPoints: Understand addition vs condensation polymerisation and examples of each.
Monomer functionality determines final polymer architecture.
Relate polymer structure to mechanical and thermal properties.
examples: Addition polymer: polyethylene from ethene monomers.
Condensation polymer: nylon from diamine + diacid (amide linkage).
examQuestions: Classify polymers with two examples each.
Explain the role of functionality in network formation.

topics.title: Plastics: Synthesis (reactions) and properties of Polyethylene (LDPE, HDPE, LLDPE, UHMWPE)

theory: Polyethylene (PE) is an addition polymer formed by polymerisation of ethene. Different polymerisation methods yield LDPE, HDPE, LLDPE and UHMWPE with distinct branching and molecular weight characteristics affecting density, crystallinity and mechanical properties.
LDPE: produced via high-pressure free-radical polymerisation — highly branched, lower density, good flexibility.
HDPE: produced using coordination catalysts (Ziegler–Natta or metallocene) — linear chains, high crystallinity and tensile strength.
LLDPE: copolymerisation with alpha-olefins to introduce short-chain branching — combines toughness and processability.
UHMWPE: extremely high molecular weight giving exceptional wear and impact resistance used in bearings and medical implants.
keyPoints: Link polymerisation route to chain structure (branching, molecular weight).
Explain how crystallinity controls density and mechanical strength.
List common applications for each grade.
examples: LDPE: plastic films and flexible packaging.
HDPE: pipes, containers; UHMWPE: high-wear components.
examQuestions: Compare LDPE and HDPE in structure and applications.
Describe the synthesis route for LDPE (brief).

topics.title: Vinyl Plastics (Condensation polymers) - Nylons, Phenol-formaldehyde resins (Bakelite) and Glyptal

theory: Vinyl plastics often refers broadly to polymers derived from vinyl monomers or to condensation-type resins used in engineering applications. Discuss nylons (polyamides) formed by condensation between diamines and diacids, phenol–formaldehyde resins (Bakelite) as early thermosetting plastics, and glyptal-type alkyd/resin systems used in coatings.
Highlight condensation polymer characteristics: step-growth mechanism, release of small molecules (water), and tendency to form cross-linked thermosets depending on monomer functionality.
keyPoints: Nylons: amide linkage formation and semicrystalline mechanical behaviour.
Bakelite: phenol + formaldehyde condensation producing a hard, heat-resistant thermoset.
Glyptal: polyester/alkyd chemistry used for paints and electrical insulation.
examples: Write a short reaction scheme for nylon formation from hexamethylene diamine and adipic acid (showing amide bond and water by-product).
Discuss one industrial use of Bakelite (insulators, molding compounds).
examQuestions: Outline the synthesis of a nylon polymer and state one application.
Why is Bakelite suitable for electrical insulators?

topics.title: Speciality Polymers: Engineering thermoplastics, Conducting polymers, Electroluminescent polymers, liquid crystalline polymers and biodegradable polymers

theory: Survey of speciality polymers emphasising function and application. Engineering thermoplastics (e.g., polycarbonate, PEEK) provide high temperature and mechanical performance; conducting polymers (polyaniline, PEDOT) introduce electrical conductivity for sensors and devices; electroluminescent polymers are used in display technologies; liquid crystalline polymers exhibit anisotropic mechanical behaviour; biodegradable polymers (PLA, PHA) break down under biological/composting conditions.
Discuss selection criteria for applications: thermal stability, conductivity, mechanical anisotropy, environmental degradation.
keyPoints: Understand why speciality polymers are chosen over commodity plastics for high-performance uses.
Know one example and primary application for each speciality class.
examples: Engineering thermoplastics: use of polycarbonate in safety helmets; conducting polymers: antistatic coatings; biodegradable polymers: compostable packaging.
examQuestions: Give two examples of engineering thermoplastics and their uses.
Describe one advantage of biodegradable polymers in environmental applications.

theory: Properties of solutions and their relevance to engineering formulations.
Acid-base concepts and pH control in industrial and laboratory settings.

topics.title: Water: Introduction and Water Quality Standards

theory: Introduction to water as an engineering material: occurrence, sources (surface and groundwater), and significance for industry and public health.
Water quality standards: WHO/ISI/IS/ local regulatory parameters and permissible limits for drinking and industrial use.
Physical, chemical and biological characteristics of water: colour, taste, turbidity, temperature, pH, conductivity, dissolved solids, hardness, dissolved oxygen, BOD, COD, microbial indicators.
keyPoints: Distinguish physical, chemical and biological parameters with examples.
Know common standards and typical limits for drinking water (e.g., turbidity, microbial counts, fluoride).
Explain why different uses (boiler feed, potable, process) require different quality controls.
examples: Interpret a water quality report: identify parameters outside permissible limits and suggest likely treatment steps.
examQuestions: List physical, chemical and biological characteristics of water and their significance.
Name three standard-setting agencies for drinking water quality and one key parameter each.

topics.title: Hardness of Water: Types, Disadvantages and EDTA Determination

theory: Definition of hardness and differentiation between temporary (carbonate) and permanent (non-carbonate) hardness.
Disadvantages of hardness in domestic and industrial contexts: scale formation, reduced soap efficiency, increased energy consumption, boiler problems.
Determination of hardness using complexometric titration with EDTA: principle, masking/unmasking (e.g., Mg with NaOH), indicators (Eriochrome Black T), procedure and calculations.
keyPoints: Remember units: mg/L as CaCO3 equivalent.
Know reagent roles: EDTA chelates Ca2+/Mg2+; buffer maintains pH ~10; indicator shows end-point shift.
Practice one standard EDTA titration calculation.

topics.title: Alkalinity of Water and Its Determination

theory: Definition of alkalinity (capacity to neutralize acid) and types: carbonate, bicarbonate and hydroxide alkalinity.
Titrimetric determination using strong acid (HCl) with indicators or potentiometric end-point; calculation of alkalinity as mg/L CaCO3.
keyPoints: Understand titration end-points for different alkalinity species (phenolphthalein and methyl orange equivalence points).
Practice conversions between meq/L and mg/L as CaCO3.
examples: Numerical: calculate alkalinity given titrant volume and normality.
examQuestions: Define alkalinity and explain its determination by titration.
Differentiate between alkalinity and hardness.

topics.title: Boiler Problems from Hard Water and Their Prevention

theory: Major boiler problems: scale and sludge formation due to precipitation of hardness salts; boiler corrosion (general and oxygen pitting); caustic embrittlement (due to concentrated NaOH in crevices); priming and foaming (carry-over of water with steam).
Prevention strategies: softening feed water, deaeration, chemical treatment (phosphate, carbonate conditioning), blowdown control, maintaining alkalinity and silica control.
keyPoints: Link specific causes to preventive measures (e.g., hardness precipitation -> softening or phosphate conditioning).
Understand signs of caustic embrittlement and operational solutions (reduce NaOH, control boiler concentration).
examples: Explain how priming occurs when high levels of dissolved solids and carry-over are present and how to prevent it via proper water chemistry and steam separators.
examQuestions: Describe scale formation mechanisms and two prevention methods.
Explain caustic embrittlement and preventive measures.

theory: Internal treatment methods treat water inside the boiler to control scale and corrosion without external softening. Methods include carbonate conditioning (phosphate treatment), colloidal conditioning (polyelectrolytes), and Calgon (sodium hexametaphosphate)/organic sequestrants to keep hardness in suspension or complexed.
Operational notes: correct dosing, sludge removal (blowdown), and monitoring of phosphate-to-alkalinity ratio.
keyPoints: Know when internal treatment is preferred (e.g., intermittent softening not available) and its limitations.
Safety and surveillance: deposit monitoring and chemical carry-over risks.
examples: Outline a simple phosphate conditioning scheme and how it prevents scale by precipitating hardness as sludge removable by blowdown.
examQuestions: Compare carbonate and phosphate conditioning for boiler scale control.
What is Calgon conditioning and where is it used?

theory: Principle of lime–soda ash softening: removal of temporary and permanent hardness by precipitation of Ca(OH)2 and Na2CO3 reactions with Ca2+/Mg2+.
Process steps: preparation of reagents, rapid mixing, flocculation, sedimentation and filtration. Control parameters: reagent dose calculation, sludge handling.
keyPoints: Be able to calculate reagent requirements for given hardness (numerical problems).
Understand advantages (cost-effectiveness for large supplies) and limitations (sludge generation, partial removal of total dissolved solids).

numericals.label: Lime–Soda Ash Numerical
numericals.problem: Calculate kg of lime and soda ash required to soften 1,000 m^3 of water with given Ca2+ and Mg2+ concentrations to a target hardness.
solutionOutline: Convert concentrations to equivalents or mg/L as CaCO3.
Use stoichiometry for precipitation reactions to estimate reagent mass.
Apply safety factor and report final reagent doses.

examQuestions: Explain the principle of lime–soda ash softening with a reagent calculation example.
List disadvantages of lime–soda process and ways to mitigate them.

theory: Zeolite softening uses sodium zeolites to exchange Na+ for Ca2+/Mg2+ in water. Regeneration with NaCl restores sodium form. Describe column operation, breakthrough curves and regeneration cycles.
Discuss ion-exchange capacity, exhaustion, and regeneration chemistry.
keyPoints: Understand operation of downflow/overhead regenerators and design parameters like bed depth and contact time.
Practice breakthrough/regeneration calculations for softening capacity.

numericals.label: Zeolite Numerical
numericals.problem: Given bed capacity and influent hardness, compute service run time before regeneration and salt requirement for regeneration.
solutionOutline: Estimate total equivalents removed until exhaustion.
Compute salt (NaCl) needed to replace hardness ions based on stoichiometry and percent efficiency.

examQuestions: Describe the zeolite softening process and outline a regeneration cycle.
Solve a basic zeolite process numerical for service run length.

topics.title: External Treatment: Ion-Exchange Resins and Deionisation

theory: Ion-exchange resins (cation and anion) are used for demineralisation/deionisation of water. Explain strong acid/strong base resins, mixed-bed deionisers and regeneration chemistry. Discuss application for high-purity water (boiler feed, electronics).
keyPoints: Know differences between zeolite softening and full deionisation (TDS removal).
Mention conductivity/purity monitoring and typical resin regeneration steps (acid/base).
examQuestions: Compare zeolite softening and ion-exchange demineralisation.
Describe mixed-bed deioniser operation briefly.

topics.title: Municipal Water Supply: Treatment and Disinfection (Break-point Chlorination)

theory: Steps in municipal water treatment: screening, sedimentation, coagulation/flocculation, filtration (rapid sand), and disinfection.

Disinfection by chlorination: chemistry of free chlorine species, combined chlorine (chloramines), and break-point chlorination principle—dose required to oxidise ammonia and achieve residual free chlorine.
keyPoints: Explain coagulation chemistry (alum) and role of pH and coagulant dosing.
Understand the chlorination curve, meaning of breakpoint and how to compute chlorine demand.
examples: Sketch and interpret a breakpoint chlorination curve; calculate chlorine dose for given ammonia concentration.
examQuestions: Describe municipal water treatment steps and the principle of break-point chlorination.
Calculate chlorine requirement to oxidise a given ammonia load (numerical).

topics.title: Desalination and Advanced Water Treatment: RO, Electrodialysis, Defluoridation

theory: Desalination overview: thermal vs membrane processes. Reverse osmosis (RO) principle—osmotic pressure, cross-flow membranes, salt rejection and recovery considerations.
Electrodialysis: ion migration through selective membranes under applied electric field for brackish water desalination.
Defluoridation methods: adsorption (activated alumina), Nalgonda process, bone char, and modern membrane/ion-exchange approaches. Discuss applicability and regeneration/disposal issues.
keyPoints: Know RO operational parameters: pressure, recovery, fouling control and pre-treatment needs.
Electrodialysis suitability for low-TDS brackish water; energy considerations compared to RO.
examples: Compare RO and electrodialysis for hypothetical brackish feed (TDS, energy).
examQuestions: Explain the principle of reverse osmosis and list its main operational challenges.
Describe one defluoridation technique and its advantages/disadvantages.

theory: Introduction to corrosion phenomena and general strategies for control in engineering systems.
Mechanisms distinguishing dry (high-temperature) and wet (electrochemical) corrosion with examples.
Definition and significance of the Pilling–Bedworth ratio for protective oxide scale formation.
Principles of galvanic coupling, galvanic series, and measures to prevent galvanic corrosion.
Corrosion of buried structures: soil resistivity, moisture, oxygen, and mitigation techniques.
Localized breakdown of passive films leading to pitting; causes, detection, and prevention.
How oxygen concentration cells produce localized corrosion and typical engineering examples.
Stress corrosion cracking (SCC): combined effects of tensile stress and corrosive environments.
Electrochemical mechanism of rusting and factors that accelerate iron oxidation.
Concept of passivation, protective oxide films and how passivity differs from active corrosion.
Overview of environmental, material, and design factors that influence corrosion rates.
General approaches: coatings, inhibitors, cathodic/anodic protection and design-based prevention.
Hot-dip galvanizing process, protective mechanism of zinc coatings and typical applications.
Tinning processes for corrosion resistance and solderability of metal surfaces.
Principles of cathodic protection and impressed current systems for pipelines and tanks.
Sacrificial anode systems (zinc/ magnesium) and application guidelines.
Electroplating fundamentals: electrolyte, anode/cathode, deposition mechanisms and common industries.

topics.title: Material Selection and Design for Corrosion Prevention

theory: How materials selection and engineering design minimize corrosion risk in systems and structures.
Introduction to sustainable chemical practices and technologies that reduce environmental impact.
Overview of the 12 principles and how they guide safer, more efficient chemical processes.
Concepts and industrial strategies aimed at eliminating waste and promoting circular economy.
Atom economy metric and its importance in assessing efficiency of synthetic routes.
Using renewable or less hazardous feedstocks in chemical manufacturing.
Replacing hazardous reagents with safer alternatives in synthesis and processing.
Green solvent selection and solvent-free methodologies to reduce environmental impact.
Techniques such as biocatalysis, flow chemistry and microwave-assisted synthesis for greener processes.
Strategies to reduce energy use in chemical manufacturing including process intensification.
Introduction to chemistry at the nanoscale and unique phenomena compared to bulk materials.
Overview of common nanomaterials, size-dependent properties and surface effects.
Top-down and bottom-up synthesis methods: sol–gel, chemical vapor deposition, hydrothermal and green routes.
Common surface and morphological characterization methods for materials science.
Brunauer–Emmett–Teller surface area analysis: principle, adsorption isotherms and interpretation.
Transmission electron microscopy basics: imaging, diffraction and resolution for nanoscale materials.
Key applications in catalysis, sensors, energy storage, medicine and environmental remediation.
